retrieved_docs.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, the glycemic index measures the ability of carbohydrates to affect blood glucose measurements. Various types of food affect the glycemic index, and the glycemic index can vary based on whether starchy foods are mixed in meals or consumed individually. Additionally, the glycemic index can also depend on how the food was processed, including the food's nutrient composition. The differing responses related to the glycemic index are related to how each food item is digested and the various factors that influence this rate. In general, the glycemic index is a system of classifications where each glycemic response is compared against a standard piece of white bread. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Resistance -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Impaired glucose tolerance or impaired fasting glucose Abnormal uric acid metabolism Dyslipidemia (increased triglycerides, decreased HDL-C, or small, dense LDL) Hemodynamic changes such as elevated blood pressure Prothrombotic factors (PAI-1, fibrinogen) Markers of inflammation (eg, C-reactive protein, white blood cell count) Endothelial dysfunction Other factors include the following: Body mass index (BMI) greater than or equal to 25 kg/m 2 Diagnosis of CVD, PCOS, NAFLD, or acanthosis nigricans A family history of T2D, hypertension, or CVD Sedentary lifestyle Non-white ethnicity Age older than 40 years

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Screening for thyroid disorders is performed at regular intervals and screening for celiac disease is typically done as well, although the frequency is not established. Regular screening for lipid disorders, microalbuminuria, and retinopathy are recommended based on the duration of diabetes. Assessment of mental health and psychosocial factors are also important. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Stents(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to 2009 epidemiology data, around 785,000 Americans suffered their first AMI, while 470,000 Americans had a recurrent MI. An estimated 195,000 "silent" heart attacks occur each year. About every 34 seconds, an American will have an AMI. In developed countries, the incidence of AMI is similar to that observed in the United States. The frequency of symptomatic CAD in ethnic immigrant populations in the United States approaches that of the white population, which supports the role of environmental factors. In France, the Mediterranean diet with the use of alcohol and its possible HDL-raising benefit, and the predominant use of monosaturated fatty acids (canola oil or olive oil) and omega-3 fatty acids, leads to a lower risk of atherogenesis with the said factors. [9] The reduced incidence of CAD and AMI is a phenomenon named the "French paradox." The Spanish cohort of the European Prospective Investigation into Cancer and Nutrition suggests that the Mediterranean diet may help lower the risk of CAD. [10] The frequency of symptomatic CAD is also very high in Great Britain, Scotland, Scandinavia, Finland, and Russia. Symptomatic CAD due to atherosclerosis is rare on the African continent. However, recent evidence points to a rapid rise in these statistics due to rapid urbanization and westernization of the rural African populations. The statistics of CAD vary significantly with race. As compared to whites, blacks have higher morbidity and mortality rates of CAD due to higher risk factors such as hypertension, obesity, metabolic syndromes, and physical inactivity. Men have a higher prevalence of CAD than women before menopause, but after menopause, the rates of CAD for both sexes are fairly similar. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and with both type 1 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). This interrelationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and are commonly observed in patients with either form of diabetes. Consequently, careful monitoring and management of lipid levels are essential components of care for individuals with diabetes to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

In addition, مرض السكري has rizikové faktory, highlighting the presence of specific risk factors that contribute to the development and progression of the disease.

retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2742
retrieved_docs.content: فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes mellitus), indicating that elevated cholesterol levels frequently coexist with this form of diabetes. In addition, ألم الصَدْر (chest pain) is clinically associated with فرط كوليسترول الدم, suggesting that patients who have high cholesterol may present with chest discomfort, which can reflect underlying cardiovascular involvement. The combined relationships imply that individuals with مرض السكري نوع 1 who also develop فرط كوليسترول الدم are at heightened risk for experiencing ألم الصَدْر and related cardiac complications, underscoring the importance of regular lipid monitoring and vigilant assessment of chest pain symptoms in the management of type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.content: the therapeutic landscape for diabetes includes insulin‑based regimens (particularly long‑acting injectable insulins and fixed‑dose combinations that pair insulin with GLP‑1 analogues) and a variety of oral and injectable non‑insulin agents—biguanides, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, and GLP‑1 analogues—each targeting different pathophysiological pathways to achieve optimal glycaemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.
